passages.explanation: Five days later, the high priest Ananias comes to Caesarea with some elders and a professional lawyer named Tertullus. They present their case against Paul before Governor Felix. Tertullus opens with heavy flattery toward Felix — praising his reforms and foresight — then gets to the charges: Paul is a troublemaker who stirs up riots among Jews all over the world. He's a ringleader of the Nazarene sect. He even tried to desecrate the temple. The Jewish leaders confirm everything Tertullus says. The charges are strategic — political agitation and temple defilement would concern a Roman governor. They've hired a professional to make Paul look like a security threat.

Felix signals Paul to speak. Paul's defence is measured and factual. He notes Felix has governed the nation for several years, so he can verify the facts. Paul went to Jerusalem twelve days ago to worship — they didn't find him arguing with anyone or stirring up a crowd, either in the temple, the synagogues, or the city. They can't prove the charges. However, Paul does admit this: he worships the God of their ancestors as a follower of the Way, which they call a sect. He believes everything written in the Law and the Prophets. He has the same hope in God as his accusers — that there will be a resurrection of both the righteous and the wicked. He strives always to keep his conscience clear before God and man. He came to Jerusalem to bring gifts to his nation and to present offerings. He was ceremonially clean when found in the temple — no crowd, no disturbance. The real accusers — the Jews from Asia who started everything — aren't even here. Paul systematically dismantles the prosecution.

Felix, who is well acquainted with the Way, adjourns the proceedings, saying he'll decide the case when Commander Lysias arrives. He orders the centurion to keep Paul under guard but with some freedom, allowing his friends to care for his needs. Several days later, Felix and his wife Drusilla (who is Jewish) send for Paul and listen to him speak about faith in Christ Jesus. As Paul discusses righteousness, self-control, and the coming judgment, Felix becomes afraid and says: that's enough for now — I'll send for you when it's convenient. He also hopes Paul will offer him a bribe, so he sends for him frequently. After two years, Felix is succeeded by Porcius Festus. Wanting to grant the Jews a favour, Felix leaves Paul in prison. Paul spends two years in Caesarean custody — not because of justice, but because of politics and greed. Felix is fascinated but never decides.
